items.q: What three notes form a major triad in root position?
items.why: Students think 'three notes = a triad'. Triads are specific: stacked thirds from the root. C major = C, E, G (skipping D and F). Consecutive scale notes are clusters, not triads.

items.q: How do you identify a major key from its key signature?
items.correct: The last sharp is the leading note (semitone below tonic); the second-to-last flat is the tonic.
items.misconception: The number of sharps or flats equals the tonic note number.
items.why: Students lack a reliable rule. For sharp keys: last sharp + 1 semitone = tonic (e.g., F sharp means G major). For flat keys: the second-to-last flat IS the tonic (or memorise: 1 flat = F major).

items.q: What does the time signature 6/8 indicate?
items.correct: Two beats per bar, each beat divided into three (compound duple).
items.misconception: Six beats per bar, all equal in stress.
items.why: Students count 6/8 as six equal beats. In practice it's felt as TWO dotted-crotchet beats subdivided into three quavers each - compound duple, not simple sextuple.

items.q: Chord V in C major is:
items.why: Correct answer: G. “A minor” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many notes are in a triad?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
